name: Rowing
instructions:
Choose 1 of the following activities - according to taste or available equipment. The goal is to increase the heart rate, sweat slightly, move the whole body and prepare the muscles for the next part of the training (mobility, activation, main part).

Great for the whole body, engages arms, back, legs and core. Start slower and gradually increase the intensity.

name: Treadmill
startingWeightNote: 5-10 min brisk walking or light jogging
instructions: Suitable for complete beginners and advanced users. Adjust the incline or speed according to your fitness level.

name: Elliptical trainer
instructions: Gentle on the joints, excellent for warming up the legs and arms. Set a moderate resistance.

name: Fitness stairs
instructions: Intensive heating of the lower body. Excellent for activating the buttocks and thighs. You can change the pace.

name: Jump lunges
instructions: Dynamic exercise for the more advanced. Significantly activates the buttocks, legs and mid-body. For beginners, replace with classic lunges.

name: Exercise with ropes
instructions: Explosive warm-up with an emphasis on the upper body. It engages the shoulders, back and mid-body. Excellent for circuit training.

name: Foot massage/activation with a mini roller or ball
instructions:
Massage using a roller (so-called self-myofascial release) serves to:
🌀 Release of muscle tension and fascia
🌀Improving blood flow and faster muscle activation
🌀Improving range of motion
🌀Reducing the risk of injury during subsequent training
It is advisable to include it after a light warm-up and before dynamic mobility. It is a good idea to massage each area for about 30-60 seconds or until the tension is relieved.

Target area: Foot

Great for foot activation, relieves flat feet, hallux valgus, heel pain.

name: Calf massage/activation
instructions:
Target area: Calf

Relieves tension after walking or running, often overloaded with a sedentary lifestyle.

name: Massage/activation - back of thigh (hamstrings)
instructions:
Target area: The back of the thighs / buttocks

It helps with mobility and prepares the legs for squats and pull-ups.

name: Massage/activation - buttocks
instructions:
Target area: The back of the thighs / buttocks

Relaxing your glutes will improve activation in exercises like squats and hip thrusts.

name: Massage/activation - shins
instructions:
Target area: Front of thighs

Helps with shin splints, suitable for e.g. runners.

name: Massage/Activation - TFL / ITB
instructions:
Target area: Front of thighs

A frequent source of tension in women, overloaded with weak activation of the buttocks. Light pressure, not through pain!

name: Massage/activation - Front thigh
instructions:
Target area: Front of thighs

Quadriceps release, suitable before squats or leg presses. If the area is very sensitive, start with less pressure.

name: Inner thigh massage/activation
instructions:
Target area: Inner thighs

Suitable for improving hip mobility and activating adductors. Take it slow and breathe deeply.

name: Lower back massage
instructions:
Target area: Lower back

Carefully! Rather use a soft roller, do not exercise through pain. The goal is gentle mobilization, not forceful pressure.

name: Massage the chest part of the back
instructions:
Target area: Back

Excellent for posture, relaxes the thoracic spine after long periods of sitting. You can bend your back slightly over the roller.

name: Neck massage
instructions:
Target area: Neck

Gentle pressure with a ball or hands. Great for tension or headaches. Don't use too much pressure, avoid the spine.

name: Extensions with an emphasis on ankle movement
instructions:
Sufficient range of motion in the ankle is key to:

🦶 Correct technique of squatting, lunges and walking
🦶Prevention of knee and back pain - with limited movement in the ankle, compensation occurs in another part of the body
🦶Improving stability and balance
🦶Reducing the risk of injury (e.g. sprain)

➡️ It is recommended to include ankle mobility especially before lower body training. Perform the exercises slowly, with control and focus on forward movement (dorsal flexion).

Target area: ankle mobility

Excellent for activating the ankles and calves. When lifting, focus on a slow, controlled movement. It will also help with balance and ankle stability. Can be performed on a mat or the edge of a step.

name: Ankle dorsiflexion with a kettlebell
instructions:
Target area: ankle mobility

Place a barbell on your knee and gently push forward. Increases range of motion and stability. Pay attention to the correct axis of the foot.

name: Endurance in a deep squat
instructions:
Target area: ankle mobility

Combines ankle and hip mobility. Make sure your heels stay on the ground. If range is limited, use a heel wedge. It will help improve your squatting position.

name: Toe tapping on the wall
instructions:
Target area: Ankle (dorsiflexion)

Try to keep the entire sole of your foot on the ground. Move only to the point where there is no heel lift. Gradually move your leg further away from the wall.

name: Deep lunge
instructions:
Well-moving hips are the basis of healthy and efficient movement. With their limited mobility, they often compensate in the knees or hips, which can lead to overload and pain.

💡 Benefits of hip mobilization:

🦵Improving range of motion in the squat, lunge and deadlift
🦵Better activation of the buttocks and reduced risk of lower back pain
🦵 Greater stability and control in movement

Recommended to include before lower body training or as part of a mobility routine.

Target area: hip mobility

Stretches the hip flexors (mainly the iliopsoas muscle). Leaning increases the intensity.

name: Seated hip rotation hands in front
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

Exercise in the 90-90 position. Actively roll your legs from one side to the other - keep your chest upright. Suitable for checking rotation in the hip.

name: Seated hip rotation In 90-90 position with hand support
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

Hand support helps balance. Actively roll your legs from one side to the other - keep your chest upright

name: Deep squat (we move the knees apart with the arms)
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

Actively staying in the lower squat position, elbows gently push the knees apart. It helps loosen the hips while keeping the torso upright. The heels stay on the ground.

name: Endurance in a deep squat
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

The exercise combines mobility of the hips, ankles and trunk stability. Make sure that the pelvis is not lowered, keep the natural curve of the back. It will help prepare the body for squats.

name: Trunk rotation in a deep squat
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

Start in a deep squat, one hand resting on the ground, the other rotating upwards. Follow the hand with your eyes. Excellent for combining hip mobility and spine rotation. Breathe slowly and consciously.

name: Dynamic frog stretching (Frog Rocking) - I move forward and backward
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

Great for opening the hips into abduction. Keep your knees wide, pelvis in neutral. Move in a controlled manner, it must not hurt in the groin.

name: Leg swings
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

A dynamic movement that activates the hip joints and prepares them for running, squats or lunges. Keep your core tight.

name: Lunges to the sides
instructions:
Target area: hip mobility

Opens the inner thighs and improves control of movement in the lateral direction. Keep your torso straight and your knee over your toe. Slowly move from one side to the other.

name: Hip mobility, CARs controlled movements
instructions:
2-3 reps each leg (1 slow circle forward and back)
Active full rotation of the hip joint. Slowly, with maximum control and without trunk compensation. Improves coordination and activation.

name: Shoulder rotation
instructions:
The shoulders and thoracic spine are key to:

🤸‍♀️ Correct body posture and technique of push and pull exercises (e.g. push-ups, push-ups, pull-ups)
🤸‍♀️Full range of motion when raising your arms above your head
🤸 ♀️ Prevention of pain in the cervical spine, shoulder blades and shoulders
🤸‍♀️Increasing the stability of the scapula and activating the deeper muscles of the torso

⚠️ Most clients (especially those who work at the computer) have stiff shoulders and a blocked chest → before strength training, the area needs to be opened and moved.

Recommended especially before upper body training, stretching and pressure exercises.

Target area: shoulder mobility

A warming and relaxing movement. Perform to the full extent, slowly. Pay attention to the flexion of the shoulder blades.

name: Dislocation of the shoulders (with bar or rubber)
instructions:
Target area: shoulder mobility

Keep your back straight and your arms bent. Basic shoulder opening exercise, very effective before pressure exercises.

name: Arm circling against the wall
instructions:
Target area: shoulder mobility

For the more advanced - activates the blade stabilizers. Keep your back and ribs against the wall, we guide your arms in an arc with minimal compensation. Great for push-ups and pull-ups.

name: CARs – controlled shoulder joint rotations
startingWeightNote: 1-2 slow circles in each direction / each arm
instructions:
Target area: shoulder mobility

Slowly guide the arm through the maximum possible range of motion, without compensating with the torso. Keep your core active. Excellent for conscious joint control.

name: Shoulder mobilization with a roller
instructions:
Target area: shoulder mobility

Opens the shoulder and chest area. Place your palms on the roller and move forward with exhalation.  Don't slouch at the hips. Also suitable as a recovery exercise.

name: Shoulder mobilization with bench support
instructions:
Target area: Mobility of shoulders & thoracic spine

Resting her hands on a bench (or chair), she pressed her chest down towards the ground. You can hold a figure eight or a bar. Pay attention to an active abdomen and smooth breathing.

name: Cat's back
instructions:
Target area: mobility of the thoracic spine

Slowly transition between slouching and arching your spine. Breathe deeply, move with the rhythm of your breath. It relaxes the spine from the neck to the hips.

name: Chest rotation on all fours - Threading the needle
instructions:
Target area: mobility of the thoracic spine

Active trunk rotation, head follows movement. Improves rotation between shoulder blades. Move slowly, with control.

name: Torso rotation on all fours from head to neck
instructions:
Target area: mobility of the thoracic spine

One hand behind the head, rotate the elbow upwards. Try to keep a stable pelvis. Suitable for pressures and pulls.

name: Side bows with a foam roller pressed under the chest
instructions:
Target area: mobility of the thoracic spine

The roller fixes the chest - the movement comes from the torso. It helps to open the chest and improve the lateral bending of the spine. Breathe into the ribs, keep the center of the body active.

name: Trunk rotation in a deep squat
instructions:
Target area: Mobility of the thoracic spine and hips

It combines spine rotation and hip mobility. Make sure your heels stay on the ground. Follow the upper arm with your eyes, don't bend your hips. Excellent for squats, presses and pull-ups.

name: Stretching forearms on all fours
instructions:
The wrists are often neglected, but in functional training and exercises with your own weight (push-ups, planks, pull-ups, presses) they carry a large load.
If their mobility is limited, there is an overload of the joints, compensation in the elbows, shoulders or cervical spine.

🔎 What wrist mobility brings:

✋Improve comfort and range when leaning on your palms
✋ It will reduce the risk of pain and joint overload
✋Enables safer execution of exercises on the ground (e.g. push-ups, planks)
✋Supports grip strength and work with a barbell, barbell or kettlebell

➡️ Include before upper body training, when working with your own weight or as part of preparation for handstands / gymnastics.

Target area: wrist mobility

The backs of the hands on the mat, gently push the elbows down. A slow, passive pull on the back of the wrist and forearm. Don't pull, it shouldn't hurt.

name: Stretching in false grip
instructions:
Target area: wrist mobility

Suitable as a preparation for push-ups, pull-ups or exercises on circles. Wrists slightly bent, palms weighted. Keep your shoulders steady.

name: Kneeling wrist extension - palms turned with fingers towards the body
instructions:
Target area: wrist mobility

A gentle pull on the wrist flexors. She pushed her torso back – the weight moves the body, not the wrist directly. Make sure your palms don't come off.

name: Transferring weight to the sides
instructions:
Target area: wrist mobility

Active exercise - dynamically transfer your body weight from one hand to the other. It will improve the natural load tolerance and get the joint fluid flowing.

name: Denial of the fist, Mobility of the wrist extensors
instructions:
Target area: wrist mobility

Close your fists, the backs of your wrists facing down. She hunched her shoulders slightly over her hands. Releases tension in the upper part of the forearms (extensors).

name: Denial of the fist, Mobility of the wrist extensors
instructions:
Target area: wrist mobility

Active mobilization. Lean your body slightly forward/backward when pushing. Keep your fists firm, the movement should come from your body, not your wrists. Also great as a prevention during strength training.

name: Breathing while lying on your back with your legs bent
instructions:
The core is not just the "abdomen", but the entire complex of muscles around the spine, ribs, pelvis and loins. It acts as a natural stabilizer that protects the spine and transfers power between the upper and lower body.

If the center of the body is inactive:

🧍‍♀️ It's harder to maintain the correct technique (e.g. in a squat or deadlift)
🧍‍♀️The body will compensate in the hips, hips or shoulders
🧍‍♀️The risk of overload or injury will increase

🌬️ Breathing activation exercises will improve the connection with the deep stabilization system (diaphragm, pelvic floor, deep abdominal muscles) and prepare the body for performance.

✅ When to activate the center of the body?
- After warm-up and mobility
- Before the main part of the training
- Or as part of a regular routine for back pain

Target area: Activating the center of the body

Place your hands on your lower ribs. During inhalation, the ribs open to the sides, exhalation activates the deep abdominal muscles. Basic breath activation (diaphragm + deep core).

name: Breath activation while lying on the stomach - crocodile breath
instructions:
Target area: Activating the center of the body

Feel the breath in your belly against the mat. A great way to engage the diaphragm.

name: Position of the child with breathing to the hips
instructions:
Target area: Core + Chest Mobility + Trunk Fascia

From the child's position, move the body to the side, extend the arms to the side. Inhale purposefully into your ribs and side, release tension with your exhale. Great at the end of activation or before an upper body workout.

name: Walking on all fours, Bear walk
instructions:
Target area: Activation + core stability

A more demanding stabilization exercise. Palms under the shoulders, knees under the hips - slow, smooth movement. Keep the tension in the abdomen and do not sag in the hips.

name: Dead bug
instructions:
Target area: Activating the deep center of the body

Arms and legs at right angles, slowly lower the opposite arm and leg. Keep your hips pressed into the mat. Slowly, exhaling as you start.

name: Active rebellion on all fours
instructions:
Target area: Core stability + strength

Palms under the shoulders, knees just above the ground. The whole body is active - shoulders, core, pelvis. Perfect before training with body weight or dumbbells.

name: Pressure of the palms against the knees on the back, Activation of the deep abdomen in the beetle position
instructions:
Target area: Activating the center of the body

Hands and knees push against each other - no movement. Exhale to activate the abdominal wall. Also suitable for beginners and clients with back pain.

name: Toe Fat (from - without lifting arms)
instructions:
Target area: Activating the center of the body

Hands fixed, lower your legs alternately and lightly "tap" the toe on the ground. Don't stick your hips out. Excellent for mastering tension in the core.

name: Lifting limbs on all fours, Superman - arms and legs
instructions:
Target area: Activating the center of the body

From a position on all fours, lift the opposite arm and leg. Pay attention to a stable pelvis, do not bend at the hips. Deep core and balance activation.

name: Crate squat
instructions:
What is it:
Movements such as squatting and lunging are among the basic human movement patterns. We use them every day – when getting up from a chair, lifting objects, walking up stairs or getting up from a squat.

✅ Why include them in training:
Develops the strength of the lower half of the body (thighs, buttocks, calves)
🏋️ They improve the stability of knees and hips
🏋️ They support coordination, mobility and balance
🏋️ They help prevent injuries (especially knees and back)
🏋️ It teaches the body to manage the load in different levels of movement

🔁 What they affect:
🏋️Strength and volume of the muscles of the lower limbs
🏋️ Mobility and mobility of hips, knees and ankles
🏋️Postural stability and posture
🏋️Ability to handle functional load in everyday life

📌 Suitable for beginners and advanced, they can be easily adapted to individual fitness (e.g. using TRX, support, or adding weight).

⚠️ What to watch out for when performing a squat:

⚠️ Foot position:
The toes point slightly outwards, the feet remain in contact with the ground all the time (including the thumb, little finger and heel).
⚠️ Knees:
They follow the direction of the tips - they do not sink in (valgus). In the lunge, the knee does not go beyond the toe significantly forward.
⚠️ Pan:
Do not lean or lean too much. The pelvis is neutral - we don't slouch, but we don't bend at the hips either.
⚠️ Back:
Keep your back straight, your core active, your head in an extension of your spine - don't look up or down.
⚠️ Squat depth:
The squat should be as deep as the mobility of the hips and ankles will allow. Quality is preferred over quantity.
⚠️ Breathing:
Inhale before moving, exhale when lifting up. Don't hold your breath.
⚠️ Pace:
Keep a controlled pace. You don't fall down, but you walk consciously and steadily.

Muscles worked: Quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, core

Level: Beginner, complete

Great for practicing proper range of motion, helps maintain control

name: Squats with TRX
instructions:
Muscles worked: Quadriceps, glutes, core

It helps to stabilize the movement and strengthen the technique

startingWeightNote: From advanced to every level (recommended for those who can do basic squats without difficulty and have good knee and trunk control)
instructions:
Muscles worked: Quadriceps, core, knee stabilizers, small muscles of the foot and ankle

Level: From advanced to every level (recommended for those who can do basic squats without difficulty and have good knee and trunk control)

A very demanding quadriceps isolation exercise, especially during the eccentric phase.
Not suitable for knee pain or poor stabilization.
Ideal as a supplement to regular squats, not as the main volume exercise.

name: Good morning (Biceps with a barbell behind the head)
instructions:
What is a hip flexor?
Hip flexion is a basic movement pattern in which the torso leans forward with minimal knee bend while the hips move backward. The spine remains in a neutral position. A typical exercise is the Romanian deadlift (RDL), swing or "good morning".

Why is it important?

🍑 Strengthens the buttocks and the back of the thighs (hamstrings), which are key for pelvic stability and back pain prevention.
🍑 Teaches the correct movement when lifting loads - i.e. instead of bending at the back, bends at the hip.
🍑 Helps improve posture and movement efficiency.
🍑 It also strengthens the deep stabilization muscles of the trunk (core).

 Muscles involved:

Gluteus maximus, Hamstrings, Erector spinae, Core, Adductors and Calves

❗ What to watch out for when exercising with hip flexion
❗Keep your back straight (do not slouch) - maintain a neutral spine without rounding or bending at the hips.
❗The movement comes from the hips, not the knees - the knees bend slightly, but the torso leans forward due to the movement in the hips.
❗Tense your stomach (core activation) - this will protect your lumbar area.
❗Movement Control – No quick or jerky movements, especially when coming back up.
❗Squeeze your buttocks when returning to the starting position - this will engage the right muscles.
❗Inhale when going down, exhale when returning up.

Muscles worked: Buttocks, hamstrings, spine erectors, core

Level: Intermediate and above

Pay attention to technique - appropriate weight, straight back, smooth movement.

name: Good Morning sitting
instructions:
Muscles worked: Buttocks, hamstrings, spine erectors

name: Attachments to trx
instructions:
What is it:
The horizontal pull is a group of exercises in which we pull the weight towards the torso in a horizontal plane - typically from front to back. This mainly includes pull-ups in a forward bend, on the TRX, rowing machines or pull-ups in a seated position.

Why include this movement in training:
◀️ Strong back = healthy back. The horizontal pull strengthens the interscapular muscles and lower trapezius, which help maintain a healthy posture.
◀️ Compensates chest dominance. Most people have overloaded pectoral muscles - horizontal pulls compensate for this imbalance.
◀️ Shoulder and shoulder blade stability support. A strong upper back improves arm control and protects the shoulder joint.
◀️ Basic movement pattern. Along with squats, presses and pull-ups, they are among the main patterns that should be part of any balanced strength program.

Muscles involved:
Primary: latissimus dorsi, trapezius, rear deltoids, rhomboids
Secondary: biceps, deep back muscles, forearm muscles, core

What to watch out for when performing:

⚠️ Keep your back straight (don't slouch).
⚠️ Do not extend your shoulders excessively forward.
⚠️ Consciously activate the shoulder blades - pull them towards you and down.
⚠️ When doing TRX or rowing, keep your body in a straight line.
⚠️ The wrists remain in a neutral position, do not break them.
⚠️Don't use inertia - movement should be controlled.

Muscles worked: back muscles, interscapularis, biceps, mid-body

Level: Beginner

The more vertical the position, the harder

instructions:
Muscles worked: back muscles, biceps, core stabilization

Level: Slightly advanced

Greater demands on balance

name: Dumbbell pull-ups in a forward bend

name: Seated back pull-ups / Lower pulley pull-ups

name: Overhand push-ups
instructions:
A vertical pull is one of the basic movement patterns in which we pull or pull resistance from top to bottom. It is one of the most effective exercises for developing the back muscles, especially the latissimus dorsi, but it also strengthens the arms, grip and core.

👉 As part of strength training, it helps:
⬇️ improve posture (e.g. extended shoulders),
⬇️ compensate for a sedentary lifestyle (hunched over the computer),
⬇️ increase functional strength for normal daily movements (e.g. lifting a child),
⬇️ prepare the body for more demanding exercises (e.g. push-ups, pull-ups on the bar).

What to watch out for when performing:
⚠️ The shoulders should be pulled down and back (not pulled towards the neck).
⚠️ Do not sag at the hips (active center of the body).
⚠️ Movement starts from the shoulder blades, not just the arms.
⚠️ When pulling the pulley: do not bend the elbows back, but towards the torso.

Choose a grip according to your abilities - a wider grip engages more of the back, a narrower more biceps.

Muscles worked: Latissimus dorsi, interscapular muscles, trapezius, biceps

Level: Advanced

The back is involved more, the grip is more demanding on strength

name: Cranks
instructions:
What is horizontal pressure?
A horizontal press is a movement where you push a weight forward from your chest – either standing, sitting or lying down. This includes, for example, push-ups, bench press or machine presses. The main difference with vertical pressures (overhead pressures) is the direction of movement.

Why is it important:
➡️ Strengthens pectoral muscles, triceps and the front part of the shoulders.
➡️ Helps improve posture, especially if you have a weak upper body.
➡️ It is essential for the overall strength of the upper half of the body and for balance with stretching exercises (e.g. pull-ups).

Muscles involved:
Chest muscles (pectoralis major and minor), Triceps brachii, Anterior deltoid (front part of the shoulders), Stabilization: core, shoulder blades

What to watch out for when performing:
⚠️ Pull your shoulders down and back to avoid overloading the shoulder joint.
⚠️ Strengthen the center of the body - you will prevent sagging in the hips.
⚠️ Elbows do not point too high (ideally approx. 45° from the body during bench press).
⚠️ Breathe smoothly - inhale when starting, exhale when pressing.

Muscles worked: Pectorals, triceps, anterior deltoid, core

Level: From a beginner

Keep your body straight, don't bend your hips.

name: Landmine shoulder presses with the axis in a semi-kneeling position
instructions:
Why include vertical presses in training?
Overhead presses are a fundamental movement for developing shoulder strength, core stability and proper shoulder blade function. We use this movement in everyday life - from putting things on top shelves to handling heavy loads. At the same time, it tests shoulder mobility, thoracic spine and trunk stability.

By regularly including overhead presses:
⬆️ you will strengthen the deltoid muscles, trapezius and deep stabilizers of the shoulder blades,
⬆️ you will improve shoulder position and body posture,
⬆️ you will increase the functional strength of the upper half of the body.

What to watch out for when performing:

⚠️ Avoid bending in the hips - activate the abdominal muscles and lower the pelvis.
⚠️ Keep your elbows under the axis/handles - don't tilt them to the sides.
⚠️ The wrist should remain in a neutral position (do not fall back).
⚠️ Don't push the range - start in a position where you are in control.
⚠️ Breathe: exhale when pressing up, inhale when lowering.

Muscles worked: anterior and middle deltoids, triceps, body collision, scapular stabilizers

Level: Beginner - Intermediate

Great for beginners - less range of motion and more control

name: Kneeling on a pulley / Kneeling in a forward bend / glute kickbacks on a pulley
instructions:
Why include lower body isolation exercises:

Isolated exercises target specific muscle groups, helping to:
🦵Improve the activation of weakened or less involved muscles (e.g. hamstrings, buttocks, calves).
🦵 Increase the volume or shape of specific parts (e.g. shaped butt, round calves).
🦵 To promote a healthy movement pattern and balance between the quadriceps, hamstrings and gluteal muscles.
🦵 Reduce the risk of overload or pain in dominant muscles during complex movements.
🦵They are ideal as supplementary exercises after the main multi-joint exercises or at the end of training.

Target area: Hamstrings

Muscles worked: Gluteus maximus (large gluteal muscle) – the main target muscle
Hamstrings – as auxiliary muscles when putting on shoes
The center of the body (core) – stabilization of the trunk and pelvis during movement)

Pay attention to the firm strengthening of the trunk, do not sag in the hips. The movement should come from the hip, not the lower back or hips. Higher engagement does not mean better activation - conscious engagement of the buttocks and controlled movement is important. Can be performed with hands resting on the machine or in a slightly forward position. Ideal at the end of training to activate the buttocks or as an isolated exercise on lower body days.

name: Face pulls
instructions:
Isolated exercises target one specific muscle or a small group of muscles and minimize the involvement of others. They are ideal for:
💪 Targeting weak or lagging muscle parts
💪 Improvement of muscle symmetry (e.g. right vs. left hand)
💪 Increase in muscle volume (hypertrophy due to higher isolated load)
💪 Prehabilitation and rehabilitation
💪 Beginners as a transition between body weight and complex exercises

Isolated exercises are not the basis of training, but are a supplement to complex movement stereotypes (squat, pull, press, hip flexion, rotation, core stability).

💡 Recommended categories for clarity:
💪 Shoulders (deltoids)
💪 Back (especially interscapulars and erectors)
💪 Chest (less movement isolation of the chest)
💪 Biceps
💪Triceps - note: you can distinguish between short vs. long head according to arm position

Muscles worked: Rear delts, trapezius, rhomboids, rotators

Great for posture, to include at the end of a back or shoulder workout

name: Slanted pulls on the pulley downwards
instructions:
Why include rotation exercises in training?
Rotational and dynamic core exercises engage the center of the body in functional movements – power transmission, trunk stabilization, spinal rotation and movement control in various planes.
While classic "core" exercises often target static stability, these movements teach the body to work in motion, respond to changes in direction, and transfer power from the lower body to the upper body - which is key both in sports and in everyday life.

Recommendations:
Include them at the end of training or as a separate core/conditioning block
Work in lower sets (2-4) and higher effort or dynamics
Alternate rotation directions, stances, and different implements (ball, disc, cable, body)

Muscles worked: Oblique abdominal muscles, serratus, shoulder blades

Standing, trunk rotation against pulley resistance, check hips

name: Tibialis Raises
instructions:
This section contains a selection of exercises that may not be part of every training session, but are very important in specific situations – such as pain prevention, unilateral load compensation, or targeted activation of weakened muscles.

🔹 They are suitable as:
🧩 supplement to the main strength training (e.g. at the beginning or end of the lesson)
🧩 part of rehabilitation or postural care
🧩 targeted activation of muscles that the client does not normally "feel"
🧩 compensation for a sedentary lifestyle

🛠 Each of these exercises has its specific place in training - if correctly included, it can significantly improve technique, stability and long-term performance.

I recommend choosing according to the needs of a specific client - for some they will be ideal as part of a warm-up, for others as regeneration or targeted activation.

Target area: Prevention of shin pain, strengthening of the front side of the lower leg

Suitable for runners, with weak tibialis anterior. Slowly lift your toes, keeping your heels on the ground.

name: Exercise with a rubber band on your fingers
instructions:
Target area: Activating the feet, improving arch control

Spread your fingers against the resistance of the rubber band. Prevention of flat feet, suitable for people with hallux valgus, for example

name: Stretching the fingers without an aid
instructions:
Target area: Foot mobility and proprioception

Toe awareness and activation – helps with arch elevation and stability while standing.

name: Seashells, activation
instructions:
Target area: Activation of the middle gluteal muscles

Important for pelvic and knee stability, especially for women. Knees apart without pelvic rotation.

name: Bridge (glute bridge)
instructions:
Target area: Activation of the buttocks and stability of the pelvis

A basic exercise to engage the glutes before training the legs. Don't slouch at the hips.

name: Bridge (glute bridge) on one leg
instructions:
Target area: Asymmetric activation of the buttocks and hamstrings

Keep the pelvis straight, the knee of the other leg extended. It can serve as an imbalance test.

name: Hang on the trapeze (passive)
instructions:
Target area: Release of the spine, shoulders, decompression

Ideal after sitting, improves posture. Arms free, relax shoulder blades.

name: Hang on the crossbar (active)
startingWeightNote: 10-15 seconds or 3-5 repetitions of the hold
instructions:
Target area: Activation of shoulder blades, preparation for push-ups

Lift your chest, pull your shoulder blades down and towards you. Teaches basic upper body control.

name: Chin tuck on mat
instructions:
Target area: Postural correction of the head and neck

Improves head and spine alignment. Suitable for slouching or neck pain. Do not push with force, just a slight backward movement of the chin.

name: External rotation both hands at the same time with strength rubber
startingWeightNote: 2-3 sets x 10-15 repetitions with slow and controlled movement
instructions:
Target area: Activation of deep shoulder stabilizers (rotator cuff). It helps to improve the position of the shoulders, prevents overloading and injuries, especially in pulling and pressing exercises. Also suitable for a sedentary lifestyle.

Include at the beginning of training as an activation and stabilization exercise before pulling or pressing exercises for the upper half of the body (e.g. push-ups, bench press). Don't use heavy resistance - the goal is control, not power.

name: Head bows, Stretching of the upper part of the trapezius muscle
instructions:
Static stretching involves a slow, controlled stretch held in a given position, usually 30-60 seconds. Unlike a dynamic warm-up, the goal is not to prepare the body for performance, but rather to calm it down, relax overworked muscles and promote regeneration.

💡 It is important to know that static stretching is not always necessary immediately after strength training. In many cases, it is more effective and pleasant to perform it later — for example, in the evening at home, or the next day as part of a recovery routine (eg after a walk, shower or mobilization exercises). This not only helps with muscle recovery, but also improves flexibility and prevents overuse.

When to recommend static stretching:

🧘‍♀️ when the client is extremely tight (e.g. hamstrings, hips, chest),
🧘‍♀️as part of an evening routine or a day without strength training,
🧘‍♀️after long sitting, traveling or one-sided load,
🧘‍♀️ during the period of increased regeneration (e.g. after a demanding training block).

Target area: Neck, back - relaxation of the upper back, prevention of neck pain

Perform slowly, without pulling the head

name: Approaching the chin to the sternum)
instructions:
Target area: Neck, back - relief for the cervical spine, stiff trapezius

Can be combined with deep breathing

name: Rotation of the head to the sides with a sideways look up
instructions:
Target area: Neck, back – stretching the oblique muscles of the neck and improving rotation

Suitable for pain from working on the PC

name: Shoulder stretch
instructions:
Target area: Shoulders – relief of tension in the shoulder girdle

Active exhalation into the pose

name: Stretching the pectoral muscles against the wall
instructions:
Target area: Chest - opening of the chest, support of correct posture

Do not rotate the torso, only the shoulder

name: Stretching of the pectoral muscles
instructions:
Target area: Chest – alleviation of shortening, suitable after pressures

name: Triceps stretch
instructions:

name: Hip flexors

Pelvis low, torso upright

name: Stretching in cobra
instructions:
Target area: Abdomen/Core – gentle stretching of the abdominal muscles, mobility of the spine

Don't tilt your head, breathe

name: Inner thighs 2
instructions:
Target area: Inner thighs - release of adductors, suitable after squats and lunges

Straight spine, slight forward bending

name:
Cross-legged seat
lean forward, Pancake
instructions:
Target area: Inner thighs - intensive stretching in a deeper range

Don't hunch your back, rather breathe into the tension

name: Quadriceps lying on side
instructions:
Target area: Front of thighs - release after squats, lunges, running

Lower the pelvis, align the knees

name: Stretching the back on the ball
instructions:
Target area: Chest + spine mobility - release after pressure or a sedentary day

Breathe freely, watch out for overloading the lumbar part

name: Buttock recumbent
instructions:
Target area: Back of thighs / buttocks - prevention of stiffness after lunges and squats

Ankle over knee, push knee out

name: Hamstring stretch with the help of a belt/rubber band
instructions:
Target area: Back of Thighs / Buttocks – improving flexibility and reducing the risk of injury

Do not raise the pelvis, the heel is directed to the ceiling

name: Standing calf stretch
instructions:
Target area: Calves - release after running, jumping or strength training

Heels on the ground, body straight

name: Calf stretch against the wall
instructions:
Target area: Calves - more intensive stretching with support

Toes on the wall, heel on the ground

name: Double knee shin stretch
instructions:
Target area: Front of thighs / shins - suitable for runners or tendon overload

More advanced, not for everyone - pay attention to the knee joints
